Exactly what revelation the scene conveys, beyond eliciting sympathy from the reader for a character who up until that point has been entirely despicable, I don't know. Felix cries. His sister, who he also happened to be carrying on an incestuous relationship, has died. This is sad, sort of, but to what end? Looking back at the essay one last time, I see Dick hints at what he wants his audience to understand, but see if you can follow his logic:

"(Buckman) is Christ himself returned, to pass judgment. And this is what he does in my novel: He passes judgment on the man sealed up in the darkness. (On whom? Taverner?) It was judged and condemned. Felix Buckman could weep at the sadness of it, but he knew that the verdict could not be disputed. And so he rode on, without turning or looking back, hearing only the shriek of fear and defeat: the cry of evil destroyed."

Only Taverner didn't kill Alys, Taverner is not convicted of killing Alys-what's more he didn't even know her in the novel's actual reality. He only met her in the novel's other reality. Is Dick saying we're guilty for the crimes we commit in both the realities he believes in? Are we judged in one world for what we do in another? I live those questions for my readers to answer. What I will say is that Dick's method has ultimately failed to convince me. His metaphor is strained and does not work. He misses, or very rarely chooses to focus on what is, or what might have been the core of an excellent novel. Instead, an ultimately empty "dream within a dream" scenario, something the professors who taught me creative writing in college told me to avoid is explored. Never end a story with "...and then I woke up," or its equivalent I was told in a stern voice by several different PhD's. With Flow My Tears, the Policeman Said, Dick does just that-and it's his zealotry for and his personal beliefs about the faulty nature of reality that have led him to such a disaster of flawed intent.

Two out of five
top_profane.title: a disaster of flawed intent

top_profane.text:
Imagine my surprise as I scrolled down a list of books on Amazon when up pops the title Teto. What? Well, the picture of the person on the cover of the book looks familiar, although its been many moons since I last saw Dick and Teto. 40 plus years ago? They were my former neighbors. Dick and Teto Heffington had been my neighbors from about 1980 to 1990. They lived four houses up the street in Brighton Hills, Montebello. Teto was the daughter of Mrs. Hol (I thought it was spelled and pronounced as Mrs. Hall.) According to my Late Grandma Kristina, Mrs. Hol had a beauty shop in Inglewood. She seemed to be a very busy woman. I didn’t know she was also a professional musician. Our houses were both built and finished in the fall of 1960. My Grandma as usual made friends with Mrs. Hol. Grandma Kristina was much more gregarious than me. Mrs. Hol had a brood of Dalmatian dogs in her backyard. They used to bark up a storm whenever I walked past. In later years, several girls in the neighborhood helped Mrs. Hol with care of her aging dogs.

I didn’t know much about Dick and Teto Heffington. They moved in after Mrs. Hol passed away. Dick was friendly. One day, I was working on something (I’ve forgotten) on my roof and Dick climbed up the ladder and joined me to supervise. Soon, I caught glimpse of Teto running down the street and she began to yell, “DICK, YOU GET DOWN FROM THERE RIGHT NOW!” I was embarrassed; but she was right, it would have been terrible if Dick had fallen off the roof. Dick and I hit it off pretty well though and I visited him and we talked about computers and his musician son.

I enjoyed Dick’s stories about driving his son Don down to Central Avenue in South Central Los Angeles. Don wanted to sit in as a drummer with the soul, blues, and R&B bands. Teto writes in her book how young Don mourned the death of John Coltrane, which I assume he first heard on the old KBCA-FM Jazz station in L.A.

Dick and Teto were ready to move on I guess by the end of the 1980‘s. The biggest thing toward the end of the ‘80‘s was when Teto put her late mother’s concert grand piano up for sale. It was huge. It filled up the entire living room of that house. But It had been well stored and it was in pristine condition. It didn’t take long to find a buyer, It was sold to a professional concert pianist. As Teto says, Mrs. Hol was indeed a very shrewd investor. After Dick and Teto sold the piano. Then, they sold the house in Brighton Hills and moved to Las Vegas. After that, I lost track of Dick and Teto.

Dick and Teto weren’t the only people I knew who were moving out of the Los Angeles Area. Later to my surprise, college friends who had become disenchanted with Las Vegas contacted me to see if I wanted to buy their houses in Las Vegas. Much of this moving by my friends to find the new Shangri-La reminded me of the country music hit by Tammy Wynette, My Elusive Dreams.

I am saddened to learn that Dick Heffington passed away. RIP. I enjoyed his stories and his warm personality and I miss my conversations with him.

By all means buy Teto’s book I wouldn’t have missed that book for the world. Her account of the ‘30‘s Depression Years is priceless. As the Scots would say, we’ll nay see her like again.

Addenda: I sadly just discovered that Teto and Dick's son Don who I mentioned in the review sadly passed away from Leukemia this past March 2021. Rest In Peace.

Addenda2

Dear Teto, I want to thank you for writing this book about you and your family’s remarkable history. I shared a very small part of it. What an incredible story you have to tell! I also missed out on your mother’s stories, but I’m afraid she had no time except to wave Hi-Goodbye as she drove on to her night job. She was a busy hard working woman. I am grateful to have known Dick for his stories and his helpful advice as I assumed more of the tasks from my father of maintaining the household. I deeply regret the losses of Dick and Don. Rest in Peace.

Be well, Richard Rollo
top_profane.title: My "lost"  former neighbor

top_profane.text:
Dick Grayson is my favorite DC hero, as nightwing and also my favorite batman. So I was a little upset when I found out that in the new 52 he wasn't a hero anymore but a secret agent, I had heard a lot of great things about it so I decided to give it a go and found myself presently surprised, I did actually enjoy the first issues but the quality really started to drop after the robin war issue, this series just felt like it didn't know what it wanted to be, dc kept trying to convince us how high the stakes were if Dick didn't succeed but all throughout the series I just didn't feel it, Dick was also very sexulized in this series and I didn't have a problem with that however those scenes made me cringe so bad that I had to put the comic down for about 10 minutes, it was just so over the top and it wouldn't had been as bad if this had happened in every 5 issues or something but no it pretty much happened in every single issue, Dick was sexulized pre-flashpoint a lot but never in such a cringe worthy or over the top way, even in the new 52 nightwing series I don't remember it being even close as bad as it is in Grayson or Batman and robin eternal and why its bothering me so much is because this is what dc writers are starting to define Dick Grayson as, just that one sexy character, Batman and robin eternal is a perfect example of that. The art for the first half is nice there's nothing special about it but its nice I guess the art for the other half sucks, that's the only word that comes to mind when I think about that art it sucks, its by far not the worst art I've seen from dc though.

Tom King and Tim Seeley's characterization for Dick was what really made me dislike this series, this Dick Grayson isn't Dick at all this. What Tim and Tom did was take fanon Dick Grayson and made him canon,this Dick Grayson is a happy go lucky, airheaded, pushover, who doesn't take anything seriously and is constantly making jokes, can't hold his own in a fight ( which was interesting considering that in one issue he took down a robot, I think it was a robot can't remember, that had powers from members of the justice league yet gets his ass handed to him by pretty much everyone ) and just wants everyone to hold hands and get along, if that's who you think Dick Grayson really is then I don't know what to think except that you've never read a pre-flashpoint comic with Dick Grayson in it and this is pretty much the conclusion I've come to with Tom King and Tim Seeley and I'm convinced that they did their research for Dick on tumblr, his supporting cast isn't that interesting except for midnighter, I really disliked their take on Helena Bertinelli, this series just became a giant mess, I can't even tell you what this series was about because I don't even know anymore, I'm glad that Dick is going to be a hero again in rebirth but I'm not sure if I'm going to be picking up nightwing rebirth considering that Tim Seeley is still going to be writing Dick and it kinds looks like continuation of Grayson in fact I don't think I'm going to be reading another batbook for a while, not until they get new writers on the batbooks, they're pretty much keeping the same group of writers that were on new 52 batbooks for rebirth and these writers just don't understand these characters. I wonder when the real Dick Grayson will return.
top_profane.title: What a mess

top_profane.text:
This review was originally posted on my website,[...]. I don't know why I didn't echo it here at the time, however I'm doing it now.

Wayne

*****

Much to my delight I was given a pre-release copy of Lawyers in Hell to review. I was disappointed that certain lawyers who I've written about in the past were inexplicably left out, but one cannot have everything.

What one can have is a lot of fun. The original review was written in two parts. On July 25, 2011 I finally got around to combining them into one piece. It's a lot neater. I really should have waited and done the review as one piece, but I was working against a time limit. The book was originally due out July 15th, then we found out that it was being shipped early, and well, Tempus Fugit.

This Is Hell

For those who are new to the series, it needs a bit of explaining. The series was originally launched by Janet Morris in 1986 with the book Heroes in Hell, and it was regarded as one of the greatest Shared Universe Fantasy series, being cited by Orson Scott Card in How to Write Science Fiction and Fantasy.

Unlike most books, the "Hell" series is about failure. This is after all Hell. Happily ever after isn't what you are going to get, and isn't what you will get. The people who you'll meet in Hell are not going to be nice people. Murderers, thieves, and scoundrels. Yes. Saints? No.

Of course murders, thieves and scoundrels do make for interesting protagonists. Very interesting protagonists. People like Gaius Julius Caesar were drawn to power in life. So was Cleopatra. Read any good translation of Josephus Flavius for a less than flattering view of the Egyptian queen.

The people in Hell are there for punishment. Which means that most of their attempts at doing anything will fail. Spectacularly. With extreme prejudice. Those that hated each other in life, will still hate each other. Back stabbing is an art form in Hell.

I think that's one of the reasons that I love this series. The level of conflict is amazing, even when there isn't any violence. It's like two cats having a stare down, both puffed up to three times normal size. Neither wants to fight. Neither is going to fight. But they both put on an act that makes it look like there will be blood all over the place, until they can back away gracefully.

I loved the original series, and wasn't happy when it went on hiatus. I was overjoyed when I heard that it was coming back, and I was even more overjoyed when Janet Morris offered me the opportunity to be able to write this review as an insider.

In a normal book review, the reviewer reads the book without any contact with the writer, or in the case of an anthology, the writers. In the first Gulf War, embedded journalists were used with the armed forces. For Lawyers in Hell I was the embedded reviewer!

I've spent the last month working inside of the project with the writers and editors, learning how they put together the books. It's a fascinating process (and no, I can't tell you the details, part of the deal is that I won't talk about some things). I've met some really neat and talented people. In some cases I've been able to help out a bit. If you do a whois on JanetMorrisandChrisMorris.Com you'll notice the odd coincidence that the website is registered in my name, and the same is true of JanetMorrisandChrisMorris.ca and JanetMorris.ca (we couldn't get JanetMorris.com unfortunately).

It's been a fun experience, and I've been invited to stick around, and continue to watch what happens as the next books in the series come out. After Lawyers will come Adventurers in Hell, Visionaries in Hell, and Swashbucklers in Hell. There is a novel in the works (yes, I know the title, but I'm holding that back), the publication date has not been finalized yet, but it will be in the first half of 2012.

I had a wonderful time reading Lawyers in Hell. Official sale date was supposed to be July 15, 2011, but it wasn't a hard release, and much to everyone's surprise books were in the stores nearly three weeks early, which is why I had to rush out the original review. And that's why the combined review doesn't match the original two part reviews which are still on the site. I had to rewrite it to bring the tenses into line. It really bothers me when one paragraph is in present tense and the next paragraph is in past tense...

I'm going to give Lawyers in Hell a FIVE star rating. Here are the reviews:

Interview with the Devil

by Janet and Chris Morris is a cautionary tale about interviewing the most powerful force for evil, and how one shouldn't try to allow one's pride to override one's common sense in the urge to get a story.

Tribe of Hell

by Janet Morris is about the native population of Hell, who regard lost souls, demons, devils, and Satan as interlopers. They act as hosts when visitors from the Heavens visit Hell, and such visitors are coming. It's also about a lost soul, who knowing of the coming visitation, intends to try to appeal his sentence.

The Rapture Elevator

by Michael Armstrong is delightful little tale about another lost soul who intends to appeal his sentence, even he knows that this is Hell, and that the rules are stacked against him.

Out of Court Settlement

by C.J. Cherryh deals with another side of the legal coin. What do you do if you want to avoid court at all costs? In this case you might just bring in the most brilliant legal mind of your era, even though your family and his were enemies in life. And if a few complications, like a Viet Cong raid on the rose gardens happen, well, it is Hell...

Revolutionary Justice

by Leo Champion is about justice of a sort. Assuming that you could have anything that could be considered justice in a place called Hell. William Walker argues with Che Guevara over who is in control of the revolution...

Tale of a Tail

by Nancy Asire starts with Field Marshal Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington, KG, KP, GCB, GCH, PC, FRS, aka The Iron Duke, measuring his grass, much to the amusement of his old foe, and next door neighbor, the Emperor Napoleon, and Napoleon's lover the Countess Marie Walewska. It then proceeds to go downhill from there. This is Hell after all :)

And Injustice For All

by Jason Cordova is about the dangers of asking for what you deserve. Especially when your name is Marie Antoinette, and you are possibly just a bit spoiled. Just a bit.

Measure of a Man

by Deborah Koren features a lawyer named Alan Bensinger who wakes up to meet William Barclay Masterson aka Bat Masterson and Wyatt Earp. And then things get really complicated...

The Adjudication of Hetty Green

by Allan F. Gillbreath is about a lawyer who is responsible for adjudicating new inhabitants of Hell. Because some cases are especially complex, and the case of Hetty Green is one of those that is. It seems that every department of Hell wants a piece of her soul, and it's his responsibility to decide whether she belongs to any of them.

Plains of Hell

by Bruce Durham stars two major Canadian historical figures, General James Wolfe and Louis-Joseph de Montcalm, who re-fight their famous battle on the Plains of Abraham in Hell on the Plains of Hell.

The Register

by Michael H. Hanson where we learn that even in Hell, honesty has its uses. Of course the people on the other side may be less than happy about your choice of weapons...

Island out of Time

by Richard Groller involves a special forces team checking out a very odd island. Pythagoras and Harry Houdini make up part of the team, and the helicopter pilot is a very, very special man.

Appellate Angel

by Edward McKeown is about a court case. Huemac the Aztec Priest is seeking remission of his sentence in hell for being an Aztec Priest, the duties of which involved tearing the hearts out of sacrifices. The lawyers have to argue both sides of the case. Is Huemac guilty of anything more than following orders?

With Enemies Like These

by David L. Burkhead. Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. tries to kidnap Lieutenant Colonel William Dunlap Simpson. The attempt fails, something goes really badly wrong, and both men end up in an afterlife that they've never seen before.

The Dark Arts

by Kimberly Richardson where Clarence Darrow represents a truly unusual client.

Heads You Loose

by Michael Z. Williamson. In this interesting little story we follow the Coordinating Legal Airborne Platoon (CLAP) as it makes a paratroop drop over Kabum, Ashcanistan, where the inhabitants don't like lawyers in death any more than they had in life, and try to show their appreciation in the time-honored way - by shooting back.

Check and Mate

by Bradley H. Sinor, in which John Adams defends Aleister Crowley on a charge of cheating at chess.

Disclaimer

by John Manning in which Monty, a new lawyer in Hell, gets his first assignment, and it's not what he was expecting.

Orientation Day

by Sarah Hulcy in which we meet the Chief Librarian of Hell's Law Library, and get to see a class of new lawyers taken through Orientation.

Remember, Remember, Hell in November

by Larry Atchley, Jr. stars one of England's most iconic figures, Guido "Guy" Fawkes. Guy is quite certain that his being in Hell is a mistake, after all, he did everything for the church...

Theo Khthonios

by Scott Oden stars one of the greatest warriors of all time, Leonidas, and his Three Hundred, the men who died with him.

Erra and the Seven

by Chris Morris in which Lysicles once again meets Erra, and in which Eshi asks a question.

And that is the last story in this volume, at which point we have to ask:

What is Hell?

Most Fantasies involve heroism. Hell is different. If a soul has made its way to hell, that soul has already lost. Thus most stories are about loosing, or about losing gracefully. Or about losing with extreme prejudice. Or escaping by the skin of your teeth.

In very few of the stories does the protagonist "win" in a classical way. This might make the stories seem brutal, or sad, but they aren't. That is one of the challenges of writing for the Hell Shared World Anthology, of finding a way of making a loss into a win.

You take characters. You give the characters choices. The choices involve good and evil, honesty and treachery, winning and loosing. The choices will affect the characters, because these characters are continuing characters.

I have beside me "Heroes in Hell" the first volume in the series, and I'm looking at the Contents page. Seven stories, four of the writers are represented in "Lawyers in Hell", writing about the same characters a quarter century later. Take "A Walk In The Park" by Nancy Asire, which stars Napoleon Bonaparte and The Duke of Wellington meeting in Hell for the first time. By the time we get to "Tale of a Tail" the two of them have become much like Oscar and Felix in the Odd Couple.

And we've got C. J. Cherryh's "The Prince", in which we are introduced to the Julian household, and it's hangers-on, which include Dante Alighieri and Nicollo Machiavelli. The stars of the show of course are Gaius Julius Caesar, Cleopatra, Hatshepsut, Sargon of Akkad, and Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus, all of which are still with us a quarter century later in "Out of Court Settlement", though there have been changes in the relationships between the characters.

Some of the characters in Lawyers are new characters. Some are old friends. Some, well, we aren't sure what the Viet Cong in DeCentral Park are, and it looks like they aren't sure what they are either.

It's been great to see the series renewed, and to see it pick up where it left off, not as a rebang. With Digital Publishing I think that Lawyers in Hell will probably be the most popular book in the series, because it will be able to reach an audience who would have never been able to find it before.

Regards

Wayne Borean

Tuesday June 28, 2011

Updated Monday July 25, 2011
top_profane.title: Wonderful Series back after a 25 year hiatus

top_profane.text: The only way to describe this book is that it is an absolute and outright sacrilege. This book is not at all what it could have (or should have) been. I'd known Paul Tibbets (commander of the 509th CBG, pilot of the ENOLA GAY to Hiroshima, Japan, August 6, 1945) for many years before he passed away. I've also known Dutch (Ted) Van Kirk, the "Silverplate" B-29 ENOLA GAY's navigator, for many years as well. There's unfortunately no comparison in quality or content to any of the books Paul and Dutch had recommended to me over the years, a few examples being THE 509TH REMEMBERED, ENOLA GAY, THE SILVERPLATE BOMBERS, etc. In essence, My True Course is just that bad. Since it was announced in 2010, I'd hoped My True Course would be able to stand next to those three books I've just mentioned, as well as other excellent aviation titles. With My True Course, Dietz was instead obsessed with finding out more and more about Dutch's family (the sister of the uncle's brother's wife's cousin's aunt's son, etc.), rather than with a book that would have been about the interesting facts and details concerning Dutch's WW2 experiences in air combat and aviation history. Dietz's obsession with Dutch's family was SO great, that it made the book a year late for publishing, which was a concern specific to Dutch's failing health at that time. As a matter of fact, Dietz was chastised BY Dutch in 2011 on several occasions, mostly for wasting so much time obsessing on his family's history, rather than finishing the book as originally intended. Dietz's interviews with Dutch were/are manipulated SO heavily away from aviation history (she would say time and again that "This isn't going to be an airplane book!"), that the book holds almost nothing of interest for the aviation history enthusiast. Even what was written concerning the Hiroshima mission (where Dutch was of course the ENOLA GAY's navigator), has been "cleansed", with any "grittiness" Dutch and I had talked about over the years deleted. Dietz's footnotes might make her appear to know what she's doing, but it's nothing more than a calculated and manipulated ruse. The 'historic' side of this pathetic book that attempts to hold the family letters together (the letters being mainly what 95% of this book is about), is nothing more than a re-write of parts of already well known (and generalized) history of the ETO and PTO in WW2. The so-called 'archives' Dietz said she used for her (as she calls it) 'research', seem to have been included for no other reason than to promote credibility where it doesn't belong. I know, since much of her aviation 'research' came from her use of my extensive aviation library. Since aviation wasn't/isn't her forte, Dietz arbitrarily chose to can the already agreed to Foreword that Richard "Dick" Cole (Jimmy Doolittle's co-pilot on the April, 1942 Tokyo raid) was going to write for My True Course. Dietz canceled Dick Cole for the Foreword, because she said she'd found someone who was more important in a literary sense(?), whatever that meant. None of us (Dutch included) had ever even thought for one moment, that Dick Cole not being an author was of any importance. It was a non-issue that was never even brought up or discussed. Having Dick Cole do the main Foreword for this book was agreed upon with Dick Cole and the Doolittle Raider's Assoc. at the Mid-Atlantic Air Museum's June, 2010 WW2 Weekend event in Reading, PA. This was a meeting I'd set up with Dietz, Dutch, Dick Cole and David Thatcher present, an obviously historic meeting, interview and agreement. Then, a year later in 2011, while Dietz was severely obsessing over her 'researching' of Dutch's family, Dietz instead chose to totally and outright disregard (blatantly ignore?) that June, 2010 meeting at Reading, PA (and the agreement) with Dick Cole and the Doolittle Raider's Association. At this point, Dietz instead modified Dick Cole's Forward (per the book) as an interview only, HEAVILY editing it. Just like that, with no prior warning or contact with any of us (not me, Dutch Van Kirk, Dick Cole, the Doolittle Raider's Association, etc.) who were involved in mine and Dutch's original project concerning Dutch's US Army Air Corps aviation history (from 1940-1947), Dietz (just out of the blue) chose to have someone else (with none of the historical aviation connection that Dick Cole and Dutch shared) do the book's Forward. Dick Cole was then put into this misrepresented book as "A Voice from the Doolittle Raid". What had happened in 2011 (much to every historic aviation enthusiast's disappointment), was that Dietz turned away from having Richard "Dick" Cole (the man who was right there in that B-25's cockpit at Doolittle's side at the beginning of the air war against Japan) be an important part at the "beginning" of this book too. I thought that this was a VERY poignant point in WW2 PTO aviation history. The sadness of it all was that Dietz just couldn't comprehend the significance of Dutch Van Kirk's and Dick Cole's historical aviation connection, disregarding it all together. It's still amazing to me that the only reason Dick Cole was in this book at all (in ANY capacity), was because I was insistent with Dutch that Dietz shouldn't be allowed to just cast Dick Cole and the Doolittle Raider's Association aside altogether. If you're an aviation enthusiast of any kind, as you read this book you'll get the feeling that much of its aviation history in relation to Dutch (or ANY kind of real and authentic aviation history ) is not just missing, but it's actually disregarded in its entirety. What came to light too late in this project, was that Dietz had never had any interest in aviation or really known about aviation history in relation to various individuals. Dietz's ignorance of aviation history was obviously the reason she stayed away from it. When I'd met Dietz at a Town of Tonawanda, NY "store front" museum (giving a talk about one of her books in front of 5 people), it was obvious that she didn't even know who Dutch Van Kirk was. Dietz was friendly and I was trusting. I thought it might be a good idea to see if she, Dutch and I could possibly realize mine and Dutch's vision of properly telling the story of Dutch's US Army Air Corps career history. What was also needed was a fully detailed history of the Hiroshima atomic bomb mission that would be told within its true historical content and context, as I have hours and hours of interviews with Dutch on the Hiroshima mission. It's sad that the seven years of Dutch's US Army Air Corps aviation history from 1940-1947 is this book's weakest link. Asking Dietz about different aviators and aviation history before she chose to just outright appropriate this project and make this book's main theme about Dutch's family letters, would produce nothing more than a blank stare. I'd have to (time and again) explain to her who some aviator or military aviation related person was, what WW2 aircraft (their types, etc.) were, and what it all meant in relation to aviation history, Dutch's 1940-1947 US Air Corps history, the 97th BG and the 509th BG. When she'd be caught in the light of reality by being questioned (just like a deer in the headlights), Dietz use to say that with the help of others, she was trying to learn the names and differences of WW2 aircraft, etc. WOW! While I thought later on that I should have "vetted" her much more extensively before inviting her to join the project, Dietz's appropriating the whole project and making sure she'd get to do it in her own way ONLY is what destroyed it. By appropriating this project to do it her way, Dietz ultimately deprived us of Dutch Van Kirk's aviation history. Dutch's health began to fail after 2007, which really lit the fire under me to try my best to find the right person to help me get this done within the context of it's original intent. Adding to the overall difficulty in doing justice to Dutch's aviation history was the fact that aviation authors I'd been contacting over the years were telling me that they wouldn't write a book based on Dutch's life, because a book about Dutch wouldn't sell. I could find no one aviation author (for several years prior to Dietz being invited by me into the project) to help me write it in a way that would help me do Dutch justice. As the project was talked about between myself, Dutch and Dietz, everything was set up for me to work with Dietz to incorporate Dutch's aviation history within the realm of the gritty authenticity Dutch and I had agreed to, but that was not to be. Dietz just began to partially ignore me (and others, such as the Doolittle Raider's Association) when it came to what had already been agreed upon. I was then totally ignored when Dietz chose to totally change the project and appropriate as hers. Unless I'm mistaken from my conversations with him, I do not think that Dutch read this book before it went to print. If I'm wrong and Dutch DID read it before it went to print, then what was published with his okay in no way does him justice. I can only assume that Dutch must have figured he had to accept what was being done, or that with Dietz's "my way or the highway" attitude, he'd have had nothing. Dutch's WW2 family correspondence (the wartime family letters) were supposed to be used only here and there, as part of the book's biographical and historically aviation related context. Dutch's censored family correspondence was not supposed to be what his biography would be based on, but that's what Dietz (totally on her own) chose to do, while ignoring everyone and everything that had been agreed to. With extensive, never ending footnotes, Dietz tries her best to assure us that this book is completely on the up and up. What was published is nothing more than a VERY expensive hardback that puts the dates of Dutch's censored WW2 family correspondence into a long and drawn-out timeline of already well-known WW2 world events. All the obvious fake reviews stating that this book is the greatest cannot hide the fact that if you've read it (if you can even finish it), you wonder a) Why did I even buy this? and b) WHAT a complete and utter disappointment! A much more apt title for this almost 600 page book would have been: "Dutch Van Kirk's Censored Family Correspondence During WW2". And after the way Dietz massacred this project's original intent, there's no way I'd ever be able to trust the accuracy of what Dietz calls her 'research'. Dietz's ignorance of aviation history teamed up with her incredible arrogance to rob us of having had a properly written, historical aviation biography of an important 20th century military aviator, Dutch Van Kirk. Considering that Van Kirk is the last surviving crew member of the ENOLA GAY, what Dietz did with this book is nothing short of a monumental disaster. ~~~ October 6, 2014: Please see comments 36 and 37. Thank you. ~~~ 8/10/15: One of Dutch's best friend's with whom he traveled the USA with for years signing books at various air shows and other venues, admitted to me a few weeks ago that before Dutch had passed away, he (Dutch) had said that a) My True Course was outright s..t, and that b) he (Dutch) should have listened to me. I was told that Dutch realized that Dietz should never have been allowed to do away with the original intent of how his (Dutch's) US Army Air Corps (and all of Dutch's) aviation history was to have been properly told. It is very sad to know that Dutch passed away feeling this way, and that he (Dutch) was manipulated into accepting something that in the end, did not do him justice. May he rest in peace.
top_profane.title: A MONUMENTAL DISASTER & AN OUTRIGHT SACRILEGE!

top_profane.text:
This is a new edition, which collects both of the previously releases graphic novels Batgirl Year One and Robin Year One. For that reason I'll go ahead and give reviews of both separately, before summing my opinion up of the volume as a whole. Despite the volume's title, Robin Year One is the story that the volume has first, so that will be reviewed first.

Robin Year One- When it comes to DC the phrase "Year One" is always going to bring to mind the story "Batman Year One", which was written by Frank Miller, chronicled Batman's origin story for a new age, and is a story that many consider to be one of the greatest Batman comics of all time. I then think that the "Year One" title was placed on this story specifically to tie it into the previous one. This is not necessarily Robin's origin story, as Dick Grayson is already Robin by the time this takes place. He is already living with Bruce Wayne, his parents are already dead, and their killer has already been taken care of. It does however take place early in Robin's career and fairly early in Batman's career as well in the grand scheme of things. Therefore, both are more...innocent than they will be at later points in their career when Dick Grayson becomes Nightwing and Bruce Wayne has not only gone through a few more partners, but has also had to suffer some painful defeats. You see the two still working their relationship out, but they are already that classic Dynamic Duo that so many adults grew up reading about and watching as children.

The artwork is a major part of this and could be said to do the heavy lifting when it comes to presenting the story as if it is taking place in the past. None of the characters are drawn too much like how they are in the modern day. Dick Grayson of course is still just a teen and is wearing the short pants and pixie boots, but even Bruce Wayne and Jim Gordon also have less defined faces and expressions, as if to say that the story is taking place in a simpler time at an earlier point in their lives. Which it is of course. Bruce, as Batman, also isn't some hulking giant. In fact, if you've seen the recently released Batman vs Superman Dawn of Justice film, i'd say that Ben Affleck as Batman in that film is a good deal larger than Bruce Wayne is drawn in this story. That, again, makes sense to me, since Ben Affleck was portraying the character fairly late in his career. Little things like that and references throughout the story, such as the type of Batmobile being driven and the lack of certain trophies in the Bat Cave, help to set the timing and mood of this tale. Tidbits are also dropped throughout of future changes that are going to be taking place in the Dynamic Duo's lives. Even now, Dick Grayson never stops talking and makes it clear that he is a strong-willed character who wishes to stand out on his own, which will eventually lead him to leave the Batman's side and become Nightwing. Even now, Bruce Wayne is portrayed as a strict mentor, who imposes various rules on Grayson in order to keep him safe and in line, which will eventually lead to the relationship between the two deteriorating. At one point, after an encounter with Two-Face goes wrong, Batman fires Dick Grayson and this will not be the first time that such a sequence plays out between the characters. What's more, after Grayson has been fired his Robin costume is placed in a case and hangs in the Bat Cave, a very clear reference to how Jason Todd's costume will eventually hang in a similar manner.

One of the strongest points of the story is the way that all of its events are being chronicled by Alfred Pennyworth, Batman's trusted butler. His monologue over the story does an excellent job explaining much of what is going on, his own worries about it, and serves to provide valuable information about the differences between Bruce and Dick, and how the two are effecting one another. I am tempted to say that you don't even really need the monologue, because the artwork and storytelling are just that good, but it does propel the story to another level. It also helps that the story isn't just one adventure that Batman and Robin are going on. It's multiple and so they face various villains, such as Two-Face, Mr. Freeze, Mad Hatter, and a few others. Personally, I love stories where that happens and they are so much more interesting than the current trend of giving readers one villain per arc. This story also chronicles a fairly important moment in Dick Grayson's career, his first encounter with Two-Face, where he is nearly beaten to death (An incident that is also not that different from how Jason Todd will eventually meet his own demise at the hands of the Joker).

This story is the perfect adventure to introduce yourself to Dick Grayson, the first Robin, and one of the most important characters in the Batman mythos. It does not feature his origin, but his origin is sufficiently covered through different conversations and images. His relationships with Bruce Wayne, Jim Gordon, and Alfred Pennyworth are all established, as is his personality as someone who wishes to stand out, but genuinely wants to do good and help Batman in his crusade. His constantly repeated line of " Somebody's gotta help him, might as well be me" is perfect for the character and captures his essence just as much as the dynamic artwork, which features him jumping around and showing off his skills as an acrobat, or the bright costume he dons every night.

Batgirl Year One- Similar to Robin Year One, the "Year One" phrase in this title instantly reminds you of Frank Miller's legendary story. In retrospect, it makes perfect sense that these two stories were paired together and I actually wonder why they've never released all three "Year One" stories together as a set. They really do go together, one featuring the origin of Batman, the second featuring an important adventure in Dick Grayson's first year as Robin, and the third featuring the origin of Batgirl. Robin Year One is probably the story that stands out the most, but all three really do go together and can easily be read one after the other. That being said, this is the perfect Barbara Gordon story. If Batman Year One is one of the greatest Batman stories of all time I would have to say the same for this story in relation to Barbara Gordon (And the same for the previous story in relation to Dick Grayson). I sometimes feel as if Barbara is often mischaracterized by many people who read about or watch her in books and television shows. There seems to be this common idea that she only became a crime fighter for the thrill of it. I think the first few pages of this story should dissuade anyone from thinking such a thing.

For one, it's instantly established that she originally wished to become a police officer, but was forbidden from doing so by her father. A stack of police academy applications with a big red "Rejection" stamp on them lets us know that she still tried to become one anyways, multiple times. This, along with an argument with her father, also lets readers know that she is willing to go against authority in order to get what she wants. Her own monologue replaces Alfred's in this story and acknowledges that she does desire to be "In on the action", but also notes that she doesn't wish to live some tedious life that other people have decided is right for her. Another scene that takes place in a dojo establishes that she already has some familiarity with the martial arts and that she's quite good at it to boot. And yet another scene taking place at FBI headquarters establishes that, even without her father's approval, she is going to go to whatever lengths are necessary to find a career that works for her. Notably, each career we see her pursue involves fighting for justice in some manner, which gives readers the impression that she is a character who wishes to do what's right and to fight for it. And all of this is just within the first 12 pages.

I've never been a major fan of Barbara Gordon as a character, but I have always found her to be similar, yet different to Batman and Robin. Like them, she strives to fight for justice. Like Robin, she uses her status to make it so that people underestimate her and then turns the tables on them. Like Batman, she is a close confidant to Commisioner (Captain in the two stories presented in this volume) Gordon. Though I don't believe she has ever attained the status of her male counterparts, there is actually quite a bit you can do with the character of Barbara Gordon as Batgirl in my opinion and this story actually does a good job of introducing other aspects of the DCU, even better than the previous two stories. Batman Year One had Catwoman appearing, with the Joker being referenced at the end. Robin Year One actually has the Joker appear, along with numerous other villains and it hints at later events that will happen to the Batman Family. Batgirl Year One actually has the JSA being mentioned and some members showing up. I think it's very important that Barbara notes that she never wanted to be Batman and that she in fact looks to Black Canary to be her trainer and mentor at first, but is shut down. This is a notable change from the characters original origins, where Batman was her idol and it perhaps makes more sense for this to be the case in the modern origin. There are also interesting facts presented here that will later be changed. Robin Year One made it clear that Barbara Gordon was Jim Gordon's niece, even though she called him dad, which would be her status quo for some time. That story also portrayed her, physically, as being fairly close in age to Dick Grayson. However, Batgirl Year One presents the character as already having graduated from college before she ever became Batgirl and being on the fast track to starting her career. Even though she graduated early this does a good deal of work to separate the two in terms of where they are in their lives, making it so that Barbara comes off as being older than Dick. This is probably another change made to modernize the characters and their relationship, but also serves as a callback to the past when Barbara was a good deal older than Dick.

As with Robin Year One, there are references to events that will take place in the character's future. Dr. Fate notes that he see's "triumph and tragedy" when he looks into her future. She is noted to have majored in "Computer Science and Data Retrieval" in college, works as a researcher at the library, and is fairly chummy with people who hack computers in their spare time. All will make her later transition to Oracle more logical. There are also easter eggs and references to previous or current events taking place in the story, such as a newspaper title that states "Curvy Cat-Burglar Claws Diamond Cache". And really all of this serves to make this story the perfect origin for the character. Barbara's motivations are explained, her personality displayed for the world to see, and it makes it easy to compare her to both Batman and Robin in terms of character. Robin Year One notes that Batman uses Bruce Wayne as something of a mask he shows the world, whereas Dick Grayson and Robin are the same person, in or out of costume. You would think that with Barbara's brighter personality she would be the same way, but the fact that she is not living with other costumed vigilantes forces her to take on more of Batman's "mask" idea.

Both stories overall: These are two of the best origin-style stories you'll ever come across. Not just for the Batman clan, but for heroes in general in my humble opinion. As i've noted about, Robin Year One isn't a straight origin, seeing as Dick is already Robin when it starts, but it works as a story taking place early in his career and does establish things about the character that his actual origin in the Batman comics didn't. If you're a fan of either character or the Batman mythos as a whole these are must-haves in your collection. DC also gave us a real treat by packaging both stories together, allowing you to easily read them back-to-back, as I did before writing this review.
top_profane.title: and is a story that many consider to be one of the greatest Batman comics of all time

top_profane.text:
This book explores the science fiction books of Philip K Dick from the perspective of Henri Bergson's philosophical analysis of morality as outlined in his work: "Two Sources of Morality and Religion." Author James Burton opens with an outline of Bergson's writings on morality, which were apparently initiated after Bergdon was requested to try and give insight into the destructive outcomes of World War One. We are introduced to the key ideas from Bergson's "Two Sources," which includes Bergson's notions of "open" and "closed" societies. Bergson describes closed societies as societies that institute rules or laws that are deemed to be unchanging, become habitual, dogmatic, mechanical and tend to try and preserve their nature from outside cultural influences. This is contrasted with open societies, that are dynamic, creative, based on principles of universal charity and accepting and integrating different aspects of "outside" culture, even if that outside culture or ways of being may alter the rules of the society. Bergson suggests that certain people who are so-called "mystics" may act as catalysts that open up those closed societies by tapping into the fundamental creative urge as experienced not only in human culture but in all biological life.

A second important idea from Bergson that is used by Burton to analyse the work of Philip K Dick, is the notion of "fabulation." Fabulation occurs when someone or society encounters some form of crisis or critical event that creates a vision or image that impregnates the world with a sense of agency. Bergson's classic example of fabulation is of a woman who was about to walk into a "gaping void" but avoids the disaster by hallucinating that she is about to enter a lift and a man is acting to prevent her entering the lift. Neither the man nor the lift exist, yet the danger is averted by this ghostly agency created by an act of unconscious fabulation. The fabulating event acts as a means of salvation, possibly self salvation interpreted as salvation by or from someone else, and acts as a means of challenging the mechanistic action and somnambulistic state that the "closed society" can induce in us.

Burton explores how Bergson's ideas on open and closed societies and the concepts of fabulation and salvation relate to many of Dick's stories and novels. Early in the book, particular analysis is given to the notions of fabulation salvation as it relates to the novels "Solar Lottery," "The World Jones Made," "Vulcans Hammer" and "Time Out of Joint." The analysis of these four stories was the best part of the book. Later chapters focus more on the notion of salvation in Dick's own mystical experiences and his attempts to understand them, as outlined, for example, in his "Exegesis" and his novel "Valis."

Bergson saw a kind of dualism between habituation of life into mere mechanism that runs counter to the creative and meaningfully emergent aspect of life. This dualism creates an internal conflict: is our nature nothing more than machine or are we transcendent beings moving beyond the mere mechanics of our flesh? Are we, in fact, really Gods having a human experience? As Burton points out throughout this book, this question is explored by Dick in many different ways. Is the protagonist in Dick's most famous novel "Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep," Rick Deckard, really a machine (replicant) who thinks he is a man? Is he a man who has become so disconnected from his own humanity that he is really acting just like a machine. Are the so-called "machines" or replicants in some or many ways more human than Deckard himself?

Philip K Dick was a master at pointing out that even the most mundane aspects of our society can induce fabulation. The artificial worlds created by technology and dogmatic societal rules, the worlds depicted in many of Dick's stories, whether they be the repressive Axis regime of "The Man in the High Castle," or the artificial "Truman Show-like" world created for the exploitation of poor Ragle Gumm in "Time Out of Joint," are worlds that create anomalies for Dick's protagonists in the form of meaningless or mundane artefacts or events. These mundane things or events become noticed by Dick's protagonists and they then often trigger fabulation effects in Dick's characters that induce a crisis of meaning and an expansion of their reality as they seemingly break out of the "closed society" they are trapped in. Dick defined the closed society as one that traps us in a false "reality," a reality that is in some sense evil, as seen in the ancient Gnostic concept of the "Demiurge" that creates a malevolent material world, its malevolence is revealed by Dick in his description of the "Black Iron Prison" mentioned in both his "Exegesis" writings and the novel "Valis." Frighteningly, Dick experienced this "Black Iron Prison" as timeless. It manifested as the increasingly closed, dogmatic and collapsing society of Ancient Rome and the in his contemporary experience of 1970s California as P K Dick's hellish prison was not just a prison in space, but in time as well. As Burton points out in this book, Dick experiences of Ancient Rome and modern California as two superimposed "realities" is a form of what neuropsychologists call "reduplicative paramnesia," the delusion that a place can exist in more than one location in space and time simultaneously. For the mystic and for many of Dick's protagonists, the wish is to break the prison of our own reality or "realities." Unfortunately, both Dick and many of his characters find that the horrors of whatever the reality they exist in, is often recapitulated in some other reality: modern California recapitulates in the form of Ancient Rome. Is it possible for us to ever break the closed society or "Black Iron Prison" of our reality, or are the alternate realties really just the same "closed society" taking on in another aspect? If one reads "The Man in the High Castle," one finds that the fictional "world-within-a-world" alternate history novel called "The Grasshopper Lies Heavy" depicts a world just as hellish as the Nazi occupied world depicted by Dick in the main narrative of "The Man in the High Castle." Dick shows us the recursive trap of the "Black Iron Prison." Bergson seemed to think that fabulation could lead to salvation from the closed society through mystical awakening to another reality, Dick however, seemed to be suggesting perhaps some of the mystical awakenings were just another replication of the delusion we were lost in before. We may be like Floyd Jones in Dick's novel "The World Jones Made" who mistakenly thinks he can see more about his future than he really can and yet is still trapped with the knowledge that he has done all this living of his reality before.

This book might be heavy going for some readers who are not into academic explorations on the philosophy of ontology and morality. However, the combination of Bergson (my favorite philosopher) and the work of PK Dick, arguably America's greatest ever sci fi novelist, provides an absolute feast for thinking. I don't give this book five stars only because some of the philosophical material, including the references to the work of Alain Badiou and Gilles Deleuze, took me a little too far afield from the actual novels of PK Dick for my liking.
top_profane.title: Bergson's Notion of Fabulation and Salvation in the Novels of PK Dick

top_profane.text:
I like Terrance Dicks' prose. I can't help it; I grew up reading Terrance Dicks novelisations. My Target collection and I were inseparable. I took those books to school; I took them on holiday with me; if I had been a member of the child slave trade, you can be sure that I would have brought a grubby copy of INFERNO down the salt mines with me.
Good heavens, I don't think I meant to say exactly all that. Yet, I must point out that I am predisposed towards liking Terrance Dicks books. It can't be helped; it's now hardwired into my brain. But it's not something I'm ashamed about, even now, as the world of original Doctor Who novels has moved beyond Uncle Terry's monthly churn-out. For every Terry failure, there's a wonderful Terry success. For every regurgitated ENDGAME, there's a fantastic TIMEWYRM: EXODUS. For every boring Benny chapter in BLOOD HARVEST, there's a fun Chicago chapter in, er, BLOOD HARVEST. Well, you get the idea. In any case, I found SHAKEDOWN to be very much in the style of previous Terrance Dicks books (which is what everyone said about every Terrance Dicks book, except, of course, for the first one). And what I mean by that is that it combines the fun and adventure of a solid, uncomplicated story with a breezy, entertaining style.
Well, before I get utterly carried away (quiet, you), I should at least make an attempt at describing this novel. It's a Sontaran/Rutan story. It separates the Doctor and companions in true Terry-style, and places them each in their own subplot. Chris and Roz are on the trail of a Rutan spy, tracking it from planet to planet, as it endeavors to stay one step ahead while on a secret mission of its own. Benny is dropped off on a university world, where her task is to study the history of the Sontaran/Rutan War. The Doctor's subplot involves him not being in the story very much, but don't mind about that because this is a Dicks novel and it moves too fast for anyone to notice or care about that.
As the introduction, back cover, inside pages, back page advertisement and photo inserts (including a wonderful shot right up Michael Wisher's nose -- thanks, lads) tell us, this is partially the novelisation of a direct-to-video story written by (who else?) Terrance Dicks. So, Terrance Dicks is novelizing a script by Terrance Dicks; did the 70s never end? Truthfully, the novelisation only takes up about sixty pages in the middle, and doesn't have a huge impact on the rest of the book (although a handful of characters do filter through). The sections before and after the novelisation are quite entertaining -- much more so than the novelisation itself.
Terrance Dicks has written so many adaptations of Doctor Who and related stories that I imagine that he must be able to do them in his sleep by now (and he probably does -- poor Mrs. Uncle Terry). But strangely enough, it's the novelisation portion of this book that drags the most. The middle sixty or so pages add absolutely nothing to the rest. Nothing, zilch, nada. It's padding, and it's not even interesting padding. The solution to the Sontaran's problem is blindingly obvious, yet they never solve it, because the story can't let them solve it. It's formulaic and boring; it utterly fails to fill me with the desire to see the original straight-to-video production. There are also two strange places where the middle section makes an oblique and Benny Adventures-like reference to the Doctor. Possibly this came from the original screenplay that had to sidestep various copyrights, but the jokes seem very much out of place inside what is now a genuine Doctor Who story.
It's always easy to tell when Dicks is getting bored by certain parts of the story. He seems to have enjoyed writing the Mega City portions (very similar to the Chicago sequences in BLOOD HARVEST). The Shakedown novelisation parts aren't given nearly the same amount of care, and, as a result, they tend to fall rather on the flat side. A few other portions are also breezed over, as if Dicks knew they were needed in order for the plot to advance, but simply couldn't be bothered fleshing them out. I can easily imagine the following occurring during a typical novel writing session: "Ah, yes, now for chapter seven. Hmmmmm, this part of the outline isn't going to be any fun to write. Really dull, in fact. Oh, I know. I'll just highlight these bits of the outline. Yeah, click on 'cut'. Now to open the novel document. Yes, I click on 'paste' now. Yes. Excellent. Well, that was an easy chapter to write. Now, what have I got cooked up in eight?"
The above paragraph may sound like a criticism, but it isn't. Terrance Dicks knows what makes a boring section, and (in SHAKEDOWN at least) he's quite skillful at happily skipping over the boring (but necessary) sections in order to get back to the fun and games. Hooray!
From a technical standpoint, the book seems lazy and almost amateurish. There are pieces that are sloppy and not having been fully thought through. Benny disappears for just about a hundred pages (the book is only two hundred thirty three pages long) because Dicks can't figure out what to do with her. But, hey, it's a hell of a lot of fun, so who cares about those minor details? I wouldn't want to read a hundred of these books, but once in a while this sort of adventure is very appealing.
top_profane.title: Shake it, Uncle Terry!

top_profane.text:
This is a fascinating overview of the world of garbology, the science of garbage. The authors begin by describing how the Garbage Project came to be, when students in a 1971 anthropology class at the University of Arizona hit upon studying garbage as a way to study people and culture. The Garbage Project has grown to become one of the leading scientific explorations into garbage, where it comes from, where it goes, and what it does when it gets there. Published results from the Garbage Project have covered such varied topics as changing patterns of meat consumption, using garbage volume to estimate population, and the relative quantities of diapers, newspapers, and plastics in landfills.
One of the most valuable contributions of the book is that it provides historical data to put garbage in perspective. Contrary to many people's beliefs, the authors argue that garbage and where to put it is not a new problem at all. They point out that one of the characteristics that make us human is that we create garbage, and we always have, back to the very first time a humanoid discovered how to create tools by chipping flint. To those who worry about our non-biodegradable trash, the authors remind us that the pottery shards of ancient archeological sites are nothing more than the indestructible refuse of yesteryear. And yet others worry about burying our trash in landfills which doesn't allow normal biodegradation to occur, but the authors point out that this also isn't new, describing an archeological dig of a putrid 2,000-year-old buried dump in Italy. Of course, the main message that the authors express is not that garbage is benign, but that the problem isn't new, and that garbage issues have been a concern since the dawn of civilization. They also point out that sanitation issues are even getting better. They give an overview of the history of garbage treatment in the US, from burying it under the kitchen floor, to building dumps, incinerators, and sanitary landfills. They point out that cities in the past weren't as clean as they are now. Cities used to produce mountains of coal dust and horse manure, and garbage would often simply sit on the corner, waiting to be dispersed by scavengers or pigs. They point out that "...ever since governments began facing up to their responsibilities, the story of the garbage problem in the industrialized world has been one of steady amelioration, of bad giving way to less bad and eventually to not quite so bad."
Scientists as well as ordinary people have very inaccurate ideas of what's in landfills today and how landfills work. Until the Garbage Project actually studied landfills, many landfill planners believed that trash would biodegrade somehow once it went into the landfill, and that the trash would eventually settle, producing large quantities of methane gas. By drilling into landfills and studying their contents, Garbage Project scientists have found that very little biodegradation actually occurs in landfills at all- -basically, only food scraps and perhaps a few lawn clippings break down. Since food scraps make up only a small portion of landfill volume, then settling and methane gas production is much less than expected.
If you think garbage is a problem, then an obvious way to address the problem is to reduce garbage volume. So what items take up the most space in our landfills? Many environmentalists would guess disposable diapers and plastics. But by meticulously analyzing landfill samples, Garbage Project scientists have determined that diapers comprise less than 2% of landfill volume and all plastics less than 20%. Paper, on the other hand, especially newspaper, doesn't compress well, doesn't biodegrade in landfill any better than plastic, and takes up 40% of landfill volume on average. Think of that next time you need to answer "Paper or plastic?" at the supermarket. By studying what's in our landfills and what actually happens to the stuff once it's been down there for a few decades, we can get better ideas about what the real garbage problems are and how to address them.
The authors suggest that the best solutions to garbage problems may be to ensure that there are economic incentives to garbage reduction. They point out that the weight and volume of packaging plastics has decreased dramatically since the 1960s- - plastic beverage bottles now weigh much less than they used to- -because it's cheaper for companies to pack and ship their items in lighter weight packaging. One of the persistent problems for garbage reduction is cities that charge a flat rate for garbage removal, rather than a per-can rate. In localities where residents pay a nominal fee for each bag of garbage to be disposed of, recycling participation rates are much higher, and garbage volume is less. The worst thing a city can do is to adopt uniform large containers for mechanized garbage collection, since garbage production magically increases to fill the space allotted to it.
After reading this book, I have a new-found respect for the Styrofoam cup and disposable diapers. I better understand why newspapers are so hard to get rid of, even through recycling. But there is one fishy result that leaves me a little suspicious- -at one point the authors argue that processed food creates less garbage than fresh food. The basis for this claim is a cross-cultural study they did in Mexico City and Arizona. They found that Mexican households produce a lot more garbage than American households, and that the larger volume is mostly attributable to the fact that Americans use processed foods where Mexicans use fresh foods. But wait, didn't the authors find in a different study that it was precisely food wastes that biodegrade in landfills, so in the end, isn't it better to produce more food scrap waste than packaging waste? This odd loose end leaves a bit of doubt in my mind that the book is entirely unbiased. But overall, I found the book incredibly informative and quite well written.
top_profane.title: One of the best books I�ve read this year

top_profane.text:
The previous volume of Dick Tracy wasn’t my favorite. By the standards set by Chester Gould I felt like it was one of his weaker efforts with Dick Tracy doing some of his worst detective work yet. By contrast this is probably my favorite volume so far. Volume 5 starts where the previous left off with a human smuggling ring. This is an extremely brutal story with men being thrown from a ship to their death to remove evidence. One thing this story reminded me was that if Dick Tracy ever asks you to help him in a case run away as fast as possible. It never goes well. Dick Tracy asks a member of the Chinese community named Tau Ming to assist in spying on the human traffickers and he’s promptly killed. In the next story a woman named Noana is asked to spy on some criminals and ends up getting waterboarded and later forced into planting bombs. Later in the book a jeweler helping Tracy gets bound, gagged and stuffed in a pipe with the intention of killing him by starvation and a woman asked to spy on her ex gets punched in the face and knocked out. In the previous volume he asked a fellow to dress as a criminal’s girlfriend to do some spy work. The disguise worked for half a second before the man was killed. Tracy has even involved Dick Tracy Jr, getting the poor lad a beating.

The Noana story was a bit silly. About half way through Dick Tracy has an abrupt mental breakdown and appears to commit suicide. Chester Gould does cheat a bit to convince the reader that Dick Tracy is truly cuckoo and later a mysterious bomb maker named Pete Reppoc shows up. The bomb makers name is a bit too clever and to the best of my knowledge this is the first time Gould uses his reverse name trick; something he would employ often in the future. The next story introduces Gould’s failed attempt at a humorous character, Brighton Spotts. When it comes to gritty detective stories there few who can match Gould but when it comes to comedy Gould is a bit lacking. This is another story with a lot of brutality but I had to laugh at some of Dick Tracy’s detective work. A desperate thief named Jojo Nidle ends up backing into the wet paint of a door frame and Tracy removes the frame to take it back to his lab for analysis. What makes it so funny is that there can be no doubt that the fellow who was in the just painted room was Nidle so all that detective work Tracy did was entirely superfluous. This is not the only time in the book where Dick Tracy’s detective work has been gratuitous and time wasting. I felt bad for the poor guy who had his door frame hacked up so Tracy could learn something he already knew.

The next story is thus far my favorite in the series. Dick Tracy tangles with a villain named Karpse who’s using slave labor to create chemical weapons to sell to foreign governments. Dick Tracy ends up getting badly injured and with his plan revealed Karpse decides to lay low and reinvent himself as a legitimate baker and ends up working for Tess Truehearts mom at her bakery. This is where the story gets very interesting as Karpse becomes quite popular as a talented baker and comes of as perhaps a legitimately decent fellow. After getting badly burned in a cooking accident Karpse is put into the same hospital as Dick Tracy but neither knows the others identity. Of the 5 volumes I’ve ready I probably enjoyed this section most of all. The next story where Dick Tracy goes to a health spa and gets entangled in a jewelry heist involving the son of the health spa owner is also a personal favorite.

The second to last story features a watershed moment with the introduction of Scardol. Scardol is very reminiscent of future villains with a distinctively appalling appearance. With dark beady eyes, a bizarre squared off chin and grotesquely huge pock marked forehead he looks as hideous outwardly as he is inside. The name Scardol, although not a description of his physical features as later villains would commonly have, is a strange name to go with this strange, ruthless villain. Like just about all Dick Tracy villains he doesn’t survive the end of the story but he is one of the most memorable villains we’ve seen. There is some silliness in this volume but it may well be my favorite thus far and I look forward to starting volume 6.
top_profane.title: The Rise of the Grotesque Villains

top_profane.text:
A story of deception and ever-deeper revelation. Excellent overall, and intriguing, but marred when the author weighs in with editorials or uses jarring devices to make his points. I couldn't resist a long review.

In 1736, five people cross the south western English countryside: a younger and older gentleman, two manservants and a maid. Supposedly the younger gentleman, Mr Bartholemew (henceforth B), is being taken in hand by his uncle Mr Brown, to visit a rich but neglected aunt from whom he might inherit; the maid will stay with the aunt. But there’s an odd touch: one of the men, Dick, is a deaf mute, mentally limited and barely mannered.

The story line (serious spoiler alert)

In fact, B is no mere gentleman but a duke’s son. The uncle is really Mr Lacy, an actor hired by B to create a cover story for his secret plans. He reveals his high birth to Lacy and says he wants to marry a young lady contrary to the wishes of both fathers. Disguised, B is to rescue her from remote Devon. The maid is her former servant, he says.

As for the manservants, Dick is B’s personal attendant, intimate since childhood; the other is the streetwise bigmouth Farthing - which Lacy himself hires at B’s behest - tasked with keeping busybodies off the scent, including any ducal spies.

But Lacy finds B more interested in arcane mathematics and philosophy than in his supposed sweetheart, especially during a strange detour to a megalithic ring of stones; oddly, B also tells Lacy the future may be discovered through mathematics. When the suspicious Lacy requests B’s true motives B is evasive, but affirms that he “seeks a meeting with someone. That much is true.” Meanwhile, Farthing tells Lacy that the demure maid looks very like a high class prostitute he saw at a London brothel, known to inflame customers with her pretense of modesty.

She is indeed that woman. But she too has been spun a tale by B. He tells her he hopes to overcome impotence by watching her have sex with the mute, and by bathing in curative waters in Devon. She is to hide all this from Lacy and Farthing. He promises her great though unspecified gain, which she assumes to be money, but in any case she’s grateful that he sprung her from her prison-like brothel (which involved more lies) to join the trip.

Things turn dark when Dick is found hanged at a crossroads and the others have disappeared. The duke commissions a lawyer, Ayscough, to learn the fate of his son. Ayscough tracks down Lacy, Farthing (real name Jones) and the prostitute/maid (real name Rebecca). The crisp and entertaining record of his interrogations makes up most of the novel.

Lacy tells all he knows to Ayscough. But Lacy had parted ways with B when his smokescreen role was done, before Dick’s death. Farthing/Jones was to have departed with him, Lacy says, but vanished the night before, leaving a note with an unlikely excuse.

Jones tells Ayscough that he fled because he’d chanced upon B’s real identity, and now planned to spy on B in the hope of reward from the duke. He secretly followed B and Rebecca as Dick led them to a high remote place: flat land partly ringed by a stony hill. The hill had a high point from which Jones looked down on them from afar. He saw the group kneel before a black haired woman dressed bizarrely in a silver jacket and breeches. He saw B lead Rebecca (at sword point) and Dick into a dark cave mouth. After some time Dick fled from the cave, followed later by Rebecca, who Jones then ran down and questioned.

The reader can, I think, take all these reported events as really happening in the story. Even the unlikely silver woman is reported separately by Jones and Rebecca. What happens after is more elusive. Jones tells a remarkable tale to Ayscough. (Though slippery, Jones is pinned down in the end by threat of execution; his account seems honest.) Rebecca, he says, told him the silver woman was one of three terrifying witches in the cave. There Rebecca was raped by Satan, who then showed her scenes of horrifying cruelty perpetrated by mortals against one another (some of which seem futuristic). She cried out to Jones about a maggot, saying it was part of a revolting scene she was made to witness. But she saw signs that she may be delivered by Jesus if she now foreswore sin, which she did, and was then able to leave the cave. After saying all this she parted from Jones.

Rebecca herself gives Ayscough very a different account (dismissing her tale to Jones as untruths). Earlier in their travels B had taken her off in the dead of night, without explanation, to the stone circle seen by Lacy. Her terror gave way to wonder as she had a revelation of a young carpenter and old whitebeard, presumed to be Jesus and his Father, standing within a light beamed down on them from above, to which the Jesus-figure points. Ayscough hears that the silver woman met later was not demonic but friendly. The “maggot” in the cave was what modern readers will recognise as a sci-fi craft. Stepping into one of its chambers Rebecca is shown screens depicting scenes of flight over a heavenly land which Rebecca calls June Eternal. The silver woman is one of three females, not witches but benign women of different generations; at one stage they merge together and then re-separate, like a representation of the Mother Goddess.

The stupefied Ayscough has still not established the most pressing issue, the fate of the duke’s son, but it’s implied he departed with the supernatural beings.

I see three themes.

The nature of holy revelation

Rebecca experienced revelations, but Fowles suggests that mortals can only absorb such visions in distorted form, through the lens of our limited, existing worldviews - and with our own ideas mixed up in them. Rebecca interprets the visions in the ring of stones in terms recognizable to her: a Jesus-figure and His whitebeard Father. Her later vision of heaven, in the sci fi craft, rigidly separates women and men: perhaps she has contributed this element of her vision too, due to memories of religious practices familiar to her, and/or due to her revulsion against sex after life as a prostitute. On their own, these could be dismissed as her fantasies. But the triple Goddess and the sci fi craft are far harder for her to pigeonhole and to us they show that the Beyond is involved.

The same issues apply to her (seemingly-incompatible) evil experiences inside the cave. The demonic forces she described to Jones are drawn from traditional images of witches and devils. Satan rapes her, as she had been repeatedly raped during her forced prostitution. But again the visions included futuristic, quasi sci-fi elements she could not have drawn from her past experience. Her own suffering was entwined with that of the human race, an idea unlikely to come from her own narrow life.

Turning all this around, the sci fi elements of the novel feel like a bone thrown to us moderns; a hook to catch us and lead us beyond ourselves. It’s as though the author is saying “here have this, something you TV-watching folk can relate to,” as our best chance to glimpse the ineffable.

Time

Secondly, the author is telling us that the supernatural exists outside of time, its revelations delivered by personages who know time’s “dark labyrinths”. B is obsessed with mathematical patterns, which we tend to see as timeless truths; B implies that he might be able to foresee the future through mathematics and astronomy, as he thinks the Stonehenge ancients could.

Perhaps to reinforce this theme, the author includes jarring references to future times: B is bald like a “skinhead”; Dick’s eyes are like “camera lenses”; future menial work will be done by “automata”.

In fact this book is very much of its time, published in the 1980s, when notions of the Mother Goddess were popular, particularly after Marion Bradley's Mists of Avalon came out in 1983. It was also a heyday for postmodern notions of reality being multiple and ultimately unknowable. A Maggot qualifies, I suppose, as a postmodern novel.

A “Christmas” story?

Rebecca becomes pregnant, apparently to Dick (unless the Devil). Rebecca is, or becomes, pure of heart; Dick is innocent. To my mind they are a bit like Mary and Joseph. Fowles also makes Rebecca mother to the real historical figure of Ann Lee, who founded a new religion, the Shaker sect ¬ one more religion founded on real revelation but losing its way in the fallible mortal world? Again, it is as if mortals are periodically shown the divine but can’t absorb it in pure form, or hold onto it.

… and then the rest

I don’t know why Dick was able to direct B to the place where the occult meeting occurred. Perhaps Dick’s innocence was required. It is suggested that B and Dick were more or less a mind/body split of the same person; how this relates to the story I don’t know. Nor can I see why Dick is rather cruelly abandoned at the end, unless just for plot purposes. I don’t for a moment imagine I’ve plumbed all the depths of this book! But as masterpieces go it has a few flaws.
top_profane.title: A marred near-masterpiece

top_profane.text:
It is with great pleasure that I see the life story of Dick Griffith has just been published. I stumbled upon Dick's exploits with his stunning wife and life companion Isabelle Galo while researching my latest book on Grand Canyon river runners.

The first river runners to run Lava Falls in Grand Canyon were the team of George Flavell and Ramon Montez in 1896 as they piloted the wooden boat PANTHON through the cataract. It took another fifty five years before a rubber raft would attempt the run. That would be Dick Griffith, credited with the first run of Lava in a rubber boat in 1951. The photographer who documented his run was Isabelle.

A year later, Dick and Isabelle were pioneering routes through the Barranca del Cobre along the Urique River in northern Mexico, and by the close of the 1950's, Dick and Isabelle had moved to Alaska, where Dick began his love affair with long walks over the Wilderness of the arctic.

While the book recounts one man's love affair with Wilderness, it also chronicles a change to the land and people of remote landscapes. Indeed, the remote Grand Canyon and Urique Barranca Dick knew are gone, and the arctic badlands now team with snow machines and sport hunters. At one point in 1999, late in Dick's wanderings, an old Eskimo woman followed Dick out of Cambridge Bay into the icy Wildlands. Dick wrote in his journal "...She wanted to go back to the land where life was not easy but a happy one and like myself she must have loved the memories of a simple basic life."

While I would have liked to hear more about Dick's travels in the Himalaya, one of "...three magic places in this world," the book is filled with enough adventures to keep anyone occupied, and author Kaylene Johnson has done a superb job of recounting Dick and Isabelle's travels.
